EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S. during the early morning hours of the 17th.  The surface low was over South Dakota as a line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing dry line that was south of the low.  The atmosphere was moderately unstable with about 2000 J/kg as the storms developed in the evening.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only about 400 J/kg with a freezing level around 13,000 feet.  The lifted index was in the -4 to -5 C range as the storms approached with 40 to 45 kts of shear available.  The LCL was quite low, around 750 meters.  CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer was 50 to 100 J/kg.  Precipitable water values were quite high, in the 1.25 to 1.5 inch range.  For the most part, the thunderstorms were not severe and produced mainly heavy rainfall.  Several reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall were received from southwest into north central Iowa.  As the line advanced eastward, a small mesoscale vorticity maximum was observed riding north along the line.  The thunderstorms became enhanced as it moved north with a line of damage extending from Clarke County north to Webster County.  Eighty two cars of a railroad train were derailed by the winds, with two of them blown off into the nearby field.  Numerous very large trees were downed and some farm buildings lost roofs.  Reports of trees and power lines being blown down continued north as the vorticity maximum lifted north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and grain bins were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the state during the day, dragging behind it a cold front that helped trigger off storms across portions of central and southern Iowa. Hail, damaging winds and a tornado occurred with the storms. The tornado moved from Monroe County into Wapello County damaging multiple farmsteads, power poles and a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet/RWIS measured 60 mph wind gust in Ottumwa within Railroad Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the state during the day, dragging behind it a cold front that helped trigger off storms across portions of central and southern Iowa. Hail, damaging winds and a tornado occurred with the storms. The tornado moved from Monroe County into Wapello County damaging multiple farmsteads, power poles and a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter report of estimated thunderstorm wind gust of 70 mph.  Also reported a large tree had fallen on a garage in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough pushed through the northern plains with an associated cold front extending southward into the central plains. Storms fired along the front to the west of Iowa in the evening and developed into a large MCS as it moved into Iowa. An MCV and a large squall line moved through northern and central Iowa during the overnight hours producing strong, damaging winds and heavy rain. Four brief tornadoes also occurred in central Iowa along the leading edge of the squall line in an area of stronger shear and higher moisture. The tornadoes mainly damaged trees and crops, however one did strike a barn and silo in rural Boone County. Another damaged traffic signals and an Iowa DNR nursery in the Ames area of Story County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured on a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS rolled through northern Iowa in the AM hours bringing with it numerous reports of high/damaging winds and some hail. The damaging winds brought down large tree limbs, power lines, large trees and damaged chicken and hog facilities. Lightning struck a house in Kossuth County damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported trees down in Estherville and SW of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS rolled through northern Iowa in the AM hours bringing with it numerous reports of high/damaging winds and some hail. The damaging winds brought down large tree limbs, power lines, large trees and damaged chicken and hog facilities. Lightning struck a house in Kossuth County damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS reported 59 mph wind gust at Estherville Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly northward moving boundary/warm front was draped across the area throughout the day.  By the evening vigorous storms fired up just north of the boundary and continued as the low level jet began to pick up.  Numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Fairly quickly after sunset, storms transitioned away from multi-cellular severe to training heavy rain producers over an area NW to SE roughly from Carroll to Bloomfield, including portions of the Des Moines metro.  Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 60 mph or higher wind gusts 7 miles south of Panora on Wagon Rd.  Wind gusts ongoing at the time of report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms from the late morning through the evening hours June 20th, produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rains.  In addition 4 tornadoes occurred.  One tornado resulted in 2 injuries when a house was destroyed.  Another person was injured by a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported tree and road sign damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was making its way across the state from the north and northwest and lit up in the afternoon around 4:30 pm to 5:00 pm just outside of the metro and progressed south and southeastward as isolated to multicell storms.  A tornado formed northwest of Creston, likely aided by interactions of the parent storm with the outflow from a nearby storm.  Otherwise, storms primarily dropped heavy rainfall and quarter sized hail, with a couple reports of golf ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported multiple large tree branches down in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms moved over Iowa, Illinois, and northern Missouri from the early morning hours through the late evening hours. Many locations saw damaging winds during this multiple round event. with locations near the city of Cedar Rapids seeing a very large hail event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Semi trailer reported blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches reported blown down.

Many locations saw damaging winds during this multiple round event. with locations near the city of Cedar Rapids seeing a very large hail event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage reported 1 to 2 miles north of Manchester, including farm buildings flattened and trees snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary was pushing through the state with ongoing convection through central Iowa through the day. As the boundary neared the area, the convection intensified and impacted central and southern Iowa in the evening to early morning hours. Two thunderstorm complexes moved through portions of central and southern Iowa with very heavy rainfall causing flash flooding. The severe thunderstorm complexes produced some hail but mainly strong damaging winds. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches in some areas with deep warm cloud depths during the event. This heavy rainfall further exacerbated conditions as some of the area had just received heavy rainfall in previous days. This led to some extended flooding for a few days and flooding on some area rivers. Several locations reported in excess of 4 inches of rain, while one location in Warren County reported over 7.50 inches of rainfall. The Emergency Manager for Marion County mentioned widespread reports of 8 to 10 inches with one report close to 12 inches of accumulated rainfall over the past few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was down on a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moved into the state in the early morning hours from Minnesota, producing heavy rainfall across large portions of north-central Iowa.  Highest amounts reported were generally in the 4-4.50 range with a 4.60 report in Winnebago county.  The MCS progressed generally S and SSE across central Iowa through the morning hours and was able to strengthen enough by the early afternoon to produce wind damage reports in far south central Iowa, including a few large trees, overturned campers, and downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 20 inch diameter tree down.  Numerous large limbs down as well with torrential rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another morning and early afternoon MCS rolled through Iowa, producing heavy rainfall, a couple of wind damage reports, and a handfull of flash flood reports.  Flash flooding reports were restricted to southern Iowa, while the heavy rain ranged from as far north and west as Webster County and down to Appanoose.  Heavy rainfall totals were generally around 2 inches, but included a 4 inch report from Prairie City in Jasper County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creston ASOS reported a 61 mph wind gust associated with the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds at a few locations in northwest Iowa on the late evening of July 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including a tree blown down on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon storms fired up ahead of and along a cold front that was entering Iowa from the northwest.  Numerous storms had severe hail, produced damaging winds, and two tornadoes. The first tornado was on the ground for several miles tracking through portions of Marion, Lucas and Monroe Counties. The second tornado formed just west of Albia and struck the town of Albia damaging several homes and businesses. Flash flooding was also reported in Davis and Monroe Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 18 inch diameter trees snapped.  Wind gust lasted from approximately 6:50pm to 7:20pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was pushing southward through the state this day, with little action to speak of through much of the state.  By the afternoon, a few storms were able to fire up along/ahead of the cold front and produced a couple of isolated severe weather reports, hail and damaging winds, in far southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor roof damage to shingles and soffit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds kicked up behind a cold front that swept through the state. Many sites saw sustained winds around 40 mph or higher, with some sites recording gusts over 55 to 60 mph. Damage was reported in the city of Algona in Kossuth County as the roof was blown off the VFW building. Additionally an RWIS site sustained damage to the observation platform as the tower was bent and toppled. A power pole was snapped in the town of Minburn in Dallas County, also in the county a window was blown out of a home in Adel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at a Road Weather Information Site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with hot and humid weather triggered an area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with isolated damaging winds caused by microbursts. Hail to the size of dimes and torrential rainfall also accompanied this cluster of storms as they moved southeast over far southeast Iowa, northeast Missouri, and west central Illinois during the late afternoon through mid evening hours of June 7, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to 5 trees totally blown down in the city of Fort Madison, Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with hot and humid weather triggered an area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with isolated damaging winds caused by microbursts. Hail to the size of dimes and torrential rainfall also accompanied this cluster of storms as they moved southeast over far southeast Iowa, northeast Missouri, and west central Illinois during the late afternoon through mid evening hours of June 7, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain fell in sheets, which occurred with strong winds over 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind and tornado damage occurred in central Iowa as a result of a low pressure system that raced across the state. Originally, the low pressure center developed in eastern/southeastern Colorado late Tuesday/early Wednesday and proceeded to quickly move east-northeast across Kansas before taking a more northeasterly turn as it approached southwest Iowa. Throughout the event, MUCAPE values were generally in the neighborhood of 500 J/kg.  Shear values were supportive with 0-1km values and 0-6km values around 35kts and 85-90kts respectively.  SRH values were also very supportive with 0-1km and 0-3km values around 350 m2/s2 and 500 m2/s2 respectively.  With the speed of the system and the ample support, resulting storms were fast moving, in excess of 40mph, and of the low topped supercell variety before transitioning into a linear system as the event progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of trees down near Adel.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a large pine tree snapped.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level low pressure system moved quickly across the central Plains during the day on November 11th. As low pressure moved from Kansas into eastern Nebraska during the day, warm and moist air was pushed northward into the region. This unseasonably warm and moist air led to increasing instability during the afternoon over western Iowa and southeast Nebraska. This combined with the strong wind field associated with the low produced an atmosphere ripe for severe weather. Thunderstorms developed along the cold front in eastern Nebraska by early afternoon, and then increased in coverage and intensity as they tracked east into western Iowa during the early afternoon. Large hail and isolated tornadoes were produced by these thunderstorms.

Widespread wind and tornado damage occurred in central Iowa as a result of a low pressure system that raced across the state. Originally, the low pressure center developed in eastern/southeastern Colorado late Tuesday/early Wednesday and proceeded to quickly move east-northeast across Kansas before taking a more northeasterly turn as it approached southwest Iowa. Throughout the event, MUCAPE values were generally in the neighborhood of 500 J/kg.  Shear values were supportive with 0-1km values and 0-6km values around 35kts and 85-90kts respectively.  SRH values were also very supportive with 0-1km and 0-3km values around 350 m2/s2 and 500 m2/s2 respectively.  With the speed of the system and the ample support, resulting storms were fast moving, in excess of 40mph, and of the low topped supercell variety before transitioning into a linear system as the event progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a small shed overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with hot and humid weather triggered an area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with isolated damaging winds caused by microbursts. Hail to the size of dimes and torrential rainfall also accompanied this cluster of storms as they moved southeast over far southeast Iowa, northeast Missouri, and west central Illinois during the late afternoon through mid evening hours of June 7, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limbs and branches were blown down, in addition to a rotten tree blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused damaging winds at several locations in northwest Iowa from just before to a little after sunrise on the morning of June 22nd. There was also one report of penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including up to 2 foot diameter trees snapped. The winds also blew down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S. during the early morning hours of the 17th.  The surface low was over South Dakota as a line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing dry line that was south of the low.  The atmosphere was moderately unstable with about 2000 J/kg as the storms developed in the evening.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only about 400 J/kg with a freezing level around 13,000 feet.  The lifted index was in the -4 to -5 C range as the storms approached with 40 to 45 kts of shear available.  The LCL was quite low, around 750 meters.  CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer was 50 to 100 J/kg.  Precipitable water values were quite high, in the 1.25 to 1.5 inch range.  For the most part, the thunderstorms were not severe and produced mainly heavy rainfall.  Several reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall were received from southwest into north central Iowa.  As the line advanced eastward, a small mesoscale vorticity maximum was observed riding north along the line.  The thunderstorms became enhanced as it moved north with a line of damage extending from Clarke County north to Webster County.  Eighty two cars of a railroad train were derailed by the winds, with two of them blown off into the nearby field.  Numerous very large trees were downed and some farm buildings lost roofs.  Reports of trees and power lines being blown down continued north as the vorticity maximum lifted north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused tree and power line damage west of Ogden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the state during the day, dragging behind it a cold front that helped trigger off storms across portions of central and southern Iowa. Hail, damaging winds and a tornado occurred with the storms. The tornado moved from Monroe County into Wapello County damaging multiple farmsteads, power poles and a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media report of narrow swath of wind damage going from NW to SE from Monroe-Wapello Road to 140th street.  Multiple farm silos and outbuildings damaged or destroyed.  Large wooden power lines leaning or snapped.  Time radar estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement report of a roof off of a house and numerous trees down in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS rolled through northern Iowa in the AM hours bringing with it numerous reports of high/damaging winds and some hail. The damaging winds brought down large tree limbs, power lines, large trees and damaged chicken and hog facilities. Lightning struck a house in Kossuth County damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported trees across road at 130th and Hickory Tree Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly northward moving boundary/warm front was draped across the area throughout the day.  By the evening vigorous storms fired up just north of the boundary and continued as the low level jet began to pick up.  Numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Fairly quickly after sunset, storms transitioned away from multi-cellular severe to training heavy rain producers over an area NW to SE roughly from Carroll to Bloomfield, including portions of the Des Moines metro.  Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 8 to 10 inch tree limbs snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was draped across the region on the afternoon and evening of June 10th with weak shear and a large amount of CAPE. Thunderstorms developed across the area producing numerous hail reports, heavy rain, and a few high winds across southern and southwest Iowa. Two funnel clouds were also noted in south central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail  and downed tree limbs were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across northeast Iowa on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and at least one tornado. The tornado destroyed one farm house near Maynard (Fayette County) causing injuries to both occupants of the house. Several other nearby farms sustained damage to crops, trees, and barns.  As the squall line moved across northeast Iowa it produced a swath of wind damage starting near Marble Rock (Floyd County) to around Guttenberg (Clayton County). Within this swath, a 65 mph wind gust was measured in Marble Rock that took down trees and power lines, around 17 farms sustained damage across southern Fayette County, and an Amish grocery store was destroyed near Garber (Clayton County). Preliminary damage estimates for Fayette County are in excess of $3 million. Locally heavy rains also produced some flash flooding with 480th Street washed out south of Stacyville (Floyd County) and Waterloo Creek came out of its banks near Dorchester (Allamakee County) within 30 minutes of the rain starting. The State of Iowa Governor issued a disaster proclamation for the following counties: Allamakee, Clayton, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek. A federal disaster declaration was made for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in Nashua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon storms fired up ahead of and along a cold front that was entering Iowa from the northwest.  Numerous storms had severe hail, produced damaging winds, and two tornadoes. The first tornado was on the ground for several miles tracking through portions of Marion, Lucas and Monroe Counties. The second tornado formed just west of Albia and struck the town of Albia damaging several homes and businesses. Flash flooding was also reported in Davis and Monroe Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported large tree snapped and fell on pickup truck.  Report via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary was pushing through the state with ongoing convection through central Iowa through the day. As the boundary neared the area, the convection intensified and impacted central and southern Iowa in the evening to early morning hours. Two thunderstorm complexes moved through portions of central and southern Iowa with very heavy rainfall causing flash flooding. The severe thunderstorm complexes produced some hail but mainly strong damaging winds. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches in some areas with deep warm cloud depths during the event. This heavy rainfall further exacerbated conditions as some of the area had just received heavy rainfall in previous days. This led to some extended flooding for a few days and flooding on some area rivers. Several locations reported in excess of 4 inches of rain, while one location in Warren County reported over 7.50 inches of rainfall. The Emergency Manager for Marion County mentioned widespread reports of 8 to 10 inches with one report close to 12 inches of accumulated rainfall over the past few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown over in town. Sweet corn was also blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A primarily heavy rain event affecting the southern half of Iowa. Storms were ongoing across portions of central Iowa during the day, with an area of low pressure centered across north central Kansas. Additional storm development occurred across southern Iowa to the north of a stationary front draped eastward from the surface low across northern Missouri. A few severe reports occurred with some large hail reported in Taylor County and damaging winds across portions of Taylor, Appanoose and Davis Counties. Flash flooding was also reported with the heavy rainfall in Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Appanoose County Sheriff Dispatch reported multiple trees down in Moulton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved across parts of the region Thursday afternoon and evening, as a cold front moved into Iowa.  One thunderstorm was severe, and produced damaging winds and large hail up to the size of quarters.  Another thunderstorm produced penny to nickel sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported estimated 60 MPH winds.  Half of a large four foot diameter tree was blown down.  In addition, an anchored dog house was blown about 15 feet away.  Pea to half inch diameter hail occurred as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved across parts of the region Thursday afternoon and evening, as a cold front moved into Iowa.  One thunderstorm was severe, and produced damaging winds and large hail up to the size of quarters.  Another thunderstorm produced penny to nickel sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported several small branches down.  Time is estimated from radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public estimated winds gusts of 50 to 60 MPH. Time is estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low lifted out to the west of Iowa with the surface low tracking northeast to near the Iowa-South Dakota border.  A warm front extended southeast into Iowa and lifted north as the dry line pushed into western Iowa during the afternoon.  The synoptic situation was fairly classic.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable, though cloudiness limited that somewhat.  CAPE values rose to 1500 to 2000 J/kg with a lifted index around -7 at the storms initiation.  Downdraft CAPE was only 300 J/kg.  There was 45 to 50 kts of effective shear however.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer was around 500 J/kg, with a freezing level around 12,000 feet.  The parameters were favorable for potential tornado development with the LCL only 750 meters and 50 to 100 J/kg of CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer with steep lapse rates.  Thunderstorms formed in an arc across western Iowa and quickly produced gusty winds and small hail.  The cells tracked north as the line lifted northeast. Numerous reports of tornadoes were received.  The reports were from the same tornado.  Debris was reported with the tornado in Carroll County.  Damage as done in the Lake City area of Calhoun County by a tornado, reported to be 1/4 mile wide by then.  The tornado touched down northwest of Lidderdale and tracked northeast, striking the west side of Lake City. The tornado continued passing through rural Calhoun County and hit some power poles on the west side of Rockwell City before lifting about 2 miles north of Rockwell City in rural Calhoun County. Much of the damage included leaning power poles, tree damage and uprooted trees. In the town of Lake City, the roofing material was removed from the High School, and the roof was removed from a double-wide manufactured home and a warehouse building north of town. The tornado was of EF1 strength with winds of 100 MPH.  The track was 23.5 miles long.  Additional thunderstorms produced hail and high winds.  An 80 MPH wind gust was reported west of Glidden, a machine shed was destroyed in Audubon County, and a weak shed was blown over in Cass County.  Winds were measured at 65 MPH in Pocahontas.  A strong storm moved through the Lake City area following the tornado and produced baseball size hail and destroyed a farm outbuilding west of Lidderdale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and poles were down just south of Van Meter near 360th and R Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms moved over Iowa, Illinois, and northern Missouri from the early morning hours through the late evening hours. Many locations saw damaging winds during this multiple round event. with locations near the city of Cedar Rapids seeing a very large hail event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely strong winds reported, with branches down and pieces of neighbors' houses and roofing material laying in front yard of the spotter.   Torrential rainfall also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front entered the state and produced heavy rainfall across a large portion of central Iowa.  The front eventually stalled, and transitioned to a northward moving warm front the next day.  Rainfall totals were generally in excess of 2 inches, with the largest reports in excess of 5 inches, resulting in some localized flash flooding.  In addition to the heavy rainfall, a lone severe hail report came early in the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated greater than 60 mph with 6 to 8 inch branches down. Some of the branches were older.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures started the month of September across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. As the upper level ridge of high pressure started to break down as a storm system moved across the northern Plains, a cold front moved into the area. With the hot and humid conditions ahead of the front, strong instability developed across the area. Thunderstorms developed along the front during the late afternoon and continued into the overnight hours. Some of these storms were severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Is there damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind and tornado damage occurred in central Iowa as a result of a low pressure system that raced across the state. Originally, the low pressure center developed in eastern/southeastern Colorado late Tuesday/early Wednesday and proceeded to quickly move east-northeast across Kansas before taking a more northeasterly turn as it approached southwest Iowa. Throughout the event, MUCAPE values were generally in the neighborhood of 500 J/kg.  Shear values were supportive with 0-1km values and 0-6km values around 35kts and 85-90kts respectively.  SRH values were also very supportive with 0-1km and 0-3km values around 350 m2/s2 and 500 m2/s2 respectively.  With the speed of the system and the ample support, resulting storms were fast moving, in excess of 40mph, and of the low topped supercell variety before transitioning into a linear system as the event progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported power poles leaning over along Highway 2 east of the Moulton turn off.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system lifted northeast across Iowa in the late afternoon and early evening hours.  A fast moving line of thunderstorms, some severe raced northeast at 50 mph to over 60 mph.   Numerous reports of damaging winds of 60 mph or greater were reported.  There were 4 confirmed EF-1 Tornadoes with the system.  Isolated reports of hail up to three quarters of an inch in diameter were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter estimated winds up to 70 MPH occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough pushed through the northern plains with an associated cold front extending southward into the central plains. Storms fired along the front to the west of Iowa in the evening and developed into a large MCS as it moved into Iowa. An MCV and a large squall line moved through northern and central Iowa during the overnight hours producing strong, damaging winds and heavy rain. Four brief tornadoes also occurred in central Iowa along the leading edge of the squall line in an area of stronger shear and higher moisture. The tornadoes mainly damaged trees and crops, however one did strike a barn and silo in rural Boone County. Another damaged traffic signals and an Iowa DNR nursery in the Ames area of Story County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of large tree limbs down was relayed by the Crawford County Dispatch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S. during the early morning hours of the 17th.  The surface low was over South Dakota as a line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing dry line that was south of the low.  The atmosphere was moderately unstable with about 2000 J/kg as the storms developed in the evening.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only about 400 J/kg with a freezing level around 13,000 feet.  The lifted index was in the -4 to -5 C range as the storms approached with 40 to 45 kts of shear available.  The LCL was quite low, around 750 meters.  CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer was 50 to 100 J/kg.  Precipitable water values were quite high, in the 1.25 to 1.5 inch range.  For the most part, the thunderstorms were not severe and produced mainly heavy rainfall.  Several reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall were received from southwest into north central Iowa.  As the line advanced eastward, a small mesoscale vorticity maximum was observed riding north along the line.  The thunderstorms became enhanced as it moved north with a line of damage extending from Clarke County north to Webster County.  Eighty two cars of a railroad train were derailed by the winds, with two of them blown off into the nearby field.  Numerous very large trees were downed and some farm buildings lost roofs.  Reports of trees and power lines being blown down continued north as the vorticity maximum lifted north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage reported with tree tops snapped, a flag pole was bent and heavy outdoor objects were moved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS rolled through northern Iowa in the AM hours bringing with it numerous reports of high/damaging winds and some hail. The damaging winds brought down large tree limbs, power lines, large trees and damaged chicken and hog facilities. Lightning struck a house in Kossuth County damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported numerous trees uprooted throughout town along with tree damage throughout the county.  Grain bins blown down as well.

The damaging winds brought down large tree limbs, power lines, large trees and damaged chicken and hog facilities. Lightning struck a house in Kossuth County damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS reported 50 knot wind gusts at Mason City Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A primarily heavy rain event affecting the southern half of Iowa. Storms were ongoing across portions of central Iowa during the day, with an area of low pressure centered across north central Kansas. Additional storm development occurred across southern Iowa to the north of a stationary front draped eastward from the surface low across northern Missouri. A few severe reports occurred with some large hail reported in Taylor County and damaging winds across portions of Taylor, Appanoose and Davis Counties. Flash flooding was also reported with the heavy rainfall in Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported numerous tree branches down in the West Grove community.  Also reported metal roof damage to a local business.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon storms fired up ahead of and along a cold front that was entering Iowa from the northwest.  Numerous storms had severe hail, produced damaging winds, and two tornadoes. The first tornado was on the ground for several miles tracking through portions of Marion, Lucas and Monroe Counties. The second tornado formed just west of Albia and struck the town of Albia damaging several homes and businesses. Flash flooding was also reported in Davis and Monroe Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across northeast Iowa on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and at least one tornado. The tornado destroyed one farm house near Maynard (Fayette County) causing injuries to both occupants of the house. Several other nearby farms sustained damage to crops, trees, and barns.  As the squall line moved across northeast Iowa it produced a swath of wind damage starting near Marble Rock (Floyd County) to around Guttenberg (Clayton County). Within this swath, a 65 mph wind gust was measured in Marble Rock that took down trees and power lines, around 17 farms sustained damage across southern Fayette County, and an Amish grocery store was destroyed near Garber (Clayton County). Preliminary damage estimates for Fayette County are in excess of $3 million. Locally heavy rains also produced some flash flooding with 480th Street washed out south of Stacyville (Floyd County) and Waterloo Creek came out of its banks near Dorchester (Allamakee County) within 30 minutes of the rain starting. The State of Iowa Governor issued a disaster proclamation for the following counties: Allamakee, Clayton, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek. A federal disaster declaration was made for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Amish grocery store near Garber was destroyed when the roof was blown off and some of the walls subsequently collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave on a warm front fired several rounds of thunderstorms in an moderately unstable air mass.  A couple of damaging wind events were reported with these storms in southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small trees and branches were down in the streets of Brighton.   Some smaller branches were of unknown diameter were down in streets. Winds were estimated at 60 MPH. This was a delayed report with the time estimated from local radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon storms fired up ahead of and along a cold front that was entering Iowa from the northwest.  Numerous storms had severe hail, produced damaging winds, and two tornadoes. The first tornado was on the ground for several miles tracking through portions of Marion, Lucas and Monroe Counties. The second tornado formed just west of Albia and struck the town of Albia damaging several homes and businesses. Flash flooding was also reported in Davis and Monroe Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported telephone pole down/on ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused damaging winds at several locations in northwest Iowa from just before to a little after sunrise on the morning of June 22nd. There was also one report of penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused damaging winds at several locations in northwest Iowa from just before to a little after sunrise on the morning of June 22nd. There was also one report of penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including branches blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including a one foot diameter tree snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including a 14 inch diameter tree snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of the 1st. Ahead of the cold front some moisture return from the southern Pains coupled with steep lapse-rates aloft to create marginal instability. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed along the cold front by late afternoon, developing into a line of thunderstorms before moving southeast through eastern Nebraska and into western Iowa. Isolated severe weather was reported along this line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer reported winds in excess of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracked east across southern Iowa during the afternoon of the 9th.  A line of thunderstorms formed from the low south along its cold front.  The atmosphere was unstable as temperatures rose into the 70s with dewpoint temperatures in the mid 60s. CAPE had increased to 2000 J/kg by early afternoon with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 500 J/kg.  The LCL was only around 750 meters in a shear environment with 50 kts of effective shear.  The freezing level was around 12,500 feet with a surface based lifted index of -7 C.    Thunderstorms formed in south central Iowa.  They produced hail up to half dollar size as they moved through Decatur, Wayne, and Lucas Counties.  In Lucas County, hail covered the ground and stripped decorative concrete from a retaining wall.  As the line of storms moved through the Albia area of Monroe County, a large tree was downed at the intersection of Highway 137 and Highway 5 caused by high winds.  Scattered damage was reported in the areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed at the intersection of Highway 137 and Highway 5 on the north side of Albia.  Scattered tree damage was reported throughout the Albia area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system moving through the central Plains brought another round of isolated severe weather to the area. Thunderstorms increased during the early afternoon along and ahead of a Pacific cold front over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa and moved almost due north. Instability was very limited across the area, but strong shear allowed for a few of the storms to produced severe weather. Most of the severe weather was in the form of strong thunderstorm winds, but some hail was also observed.

Most of the severe weather was in the form of strong thunderstorm winds, but some hail was also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux COOP Observer estimated winds to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of May 7th. As these storms moved across the area, they produced hail and straight line damaging winds. The largest hail reported was nickel sized in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County), West Union and Fayette (Fayette County). A barn near West Union was damaged by the straight line winds and in St. Ansgar a large branch was blown down onto a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn near West Union was damaged by the wind gusts from a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of May 7th. As these storms moved across the area, they produced hail and straight line damaging winds. The largest hail reported was nickel sized in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County), West Union and Fayette (Fayette County). A barn near West Union was damaged by the straight line winds and in St. Ansgar a large branch was blown down onto a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down, landing on and damaging a house in St. Ansgar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area was located over eastern Colorado during the afternoon of the 18th.  Moisture streamed north ahead of it with marginal instability over the state.  CAPE rose to around 1000 J/kg by mid afternoon.  The non-severe tornado index was elevated from southeast into parts of central and north central Iowa.  The freezing level was quite low, around 10,000 feet, however the CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only around 200 J/kg.  Thunderstorms formed in the afternoon and though most remained below severe levels, there were a few severe reports.  One storm produced quarter size hail that covered the ground in Decatur County.  Another storm produced an apparent microburst that destroyed a machine shed and blew it 600 feet into a field in Marshall County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A machine shed was destroyed and debris was blown 600 feet to the northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold frontal boundary moved into Iowa from the northwest.  The atmosphere was not overly favorable for severe weather.  CAPE was between 1000 and 2000 J/kg, but the atmosphere was weakly sheared with 20 to 25 kts of effective shear.  Little downdraft CAPE was available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was 400 to 500 J/kg with the freezing level around 11,500 feet.  Thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front.  A few of them produced hail and gusty winds.  A 60 MPH wind gust was reported south of the Mason City area in Cerro Gordo County.  Nickel size hail fell in Franklin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car driver had to pull over due to the strong winds.  Dime size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same system that caused the severe weather the night before continued to move northeast.  A cold front from the low in northwest Minnesota moved into the state.  Thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front.  Most of the stronger convection was to the north, however the southern extent of the storms reached into Worth County and produced a 63 MPH wind in Grafton. Strong and gusty winds were also reported across much of the area with a tightened pressure gradient in the area and strong winds mixing down from aloft. One of the stronger gusts blew a tree down in Carroll County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor tree branch damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front sagged southward through western Iowa and eastern Nebraska during the day on August 2nd. Ahead of the front during the early morning hours the nocturnal low-level jet increased ahead of the front. This allowed for isolated thunderstorms to develop in northeast Nebraska and move into west central Iowa during the early morning hours. Some of these thunderstorms reached severe limits with large hail the main threat. These thunderstorms dissipated in the morning hours, but as the instability increased during the afternoon thunderstorms redeveloped along the cold front. Vertical wind shear also increased during the afternoon as well with some of the thunderstorms becoming supercells producing large hail and damaging winds. The thunderstorms weakened by mid to late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images from social media posts indicate that a downburst occurred in Thurman. Several large tree branches were blown down, and one tree was completely snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving quickly through the northern Plains allowed for a seasonably strong cold front to sweep through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa late in the day on the 9th of August. Isolated to scattered supercells developed along the cold front as it moved southeast across the area with some of the supercells producing severe weather in the form of large hail and damaging winds, as well as locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree limbs were reported blown down in town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A healthy 15 year old maple tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper-level storm system moved across the northern Plains during the day on August 22nd. This system allowed for a strong cold front to sweep across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the late afternoon and evening. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed along the cold front from west central Iowa into eastern Nebraska, and moved southeast through the evening. Although the large majority of these storms were sub-severe, isolated reports of large hail and damaging winds were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media posts from a storm chaser in the area indicated that large street signs had been blown over along highway 34 to the east of Interstate 29. The storm chaser also reported tree damage in the area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the state late in the afternoon from the north and in doing so a few storms ignited along and near the front.  The storms progressed south and southeast through the evening and into the overnight hours.  A handful of severe reports, hail and wind, came in and were spread out across northern, central, and western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager estimated winds at over 60 mph with pea sized hail.  No damage was noted at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong storm system lifted northward from Iowa into Minnesota on August 18th. This caused a warm front to lift northward across eastern Iowa and warm and moist air to flood into the region. Showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa during the afternoon and evening hours. These thunderstorms produced an EF-0 tornado 3 miles northwest of Stanwood in Cedar County. The storms also produced thunderstorm wind damage in Jones and Des Moines Counties along with hail the size of peas. Torrential rains of 2 to 3 inches caused flash flooding in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported several trees down in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that was slowly moving eastward across Iowa. Thunderstorms in Jackson and Scott Counties produced damaging winds estimated to be near 60 mph. A thunderstorm in Louisa County caused a fire at a machine shed from a lightning strike. Brief torrential downpours were reported with several of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS Employee reported about 4 to 5 inch diameter tree limbs blown down on the southwest side of town with several smaller branches down. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that was slowly moving eastward across Iowa. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated that thunderstorm winds were gusting to 60 mph. The gusts blew down some 2 inch diameter tree branches. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS Employee reported an estimated wind gust to be 60 MPH and blew down a 3 to 4 inch diameter tree branch in neighbor's yard. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was making its way across the state from the north and northwest and lit up in the afternoon around 4:30 pm to 5:00 pm just outside of the metro and progressed south and southeastward as isolated to multicell storms.  A tornado formed northwest of Creston, likely aided by interactions of the parent storm with the outflow from a nearby storm.  Otherwise, storms primarily dropped heavy rainfall and quarter sized hail, with a couple reports of golf ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a semi truck blown over in Costco parking lot.  Time estimated via radar and picture submitted via twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind and tornado damage occurred in central Iowa as a result of a low pressure system that raced across the state. Originally, the low pressure center developed in eastern/southeastern Colorado late Tuesday/early Wednesday and proceeded to quickly move east-northeast across Kansas before taking a more northeasterly turn as it approached southwest Iowa. Throughout the event, MUCAPE values were generally in the neighborhood of 500 J/kg.  Shear values were supportive with 0-1km values and 0-6km values around 35kts and 85-90kts respectively.  SRH values were also very supportive with 0-1km and 0-3km values around 350 m2/s2 and 500 m2/s2 respectively.  With the speed of the system and the ample support, resulting storms were fast moving, in excess of 40mph, and of the low topped supercell variety before transitioning into a linear system as the event progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KWWL reported a roof blown off of a hay barn, minor damage to the siding of a house, and minor roof damage to another building.  Delayed report.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with hot and humid weather triggered an area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with isolated damaging winds caused by microbursts. Hail to the size of dimes and torrential rainfall also accompanied this cluster of storms as they moved southeast over far southeast Iowa, northeast Missouri, and west central Illinois during the late afternoon through mid evening hours of June 7, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi trailer was blown over near intersection of Highway 2 and 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds kicked up behind a cold front that swept through the state. Many sites saw sustained winds around 40 mph or higher, with some sites recording gusts over 55 to 60 mph. Damage was reported in the city of Algona in Kossuth County as the roof was blown off the VFW building. Additionally an RWIS site sustained damage to the observation platform as the tower was bent and toppled. A power pole was snapped in the town of Minburn in Dallas County, also in the county a window was blown out of a home in Adel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and hail in Ida County of northwest Iowa during the afternoon of June 6th. The thunderstorms continued into the evening and produced damaging winds near Le Mars in Plymouth County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and hail in Ida County of northwest Iowa during the afternoon of June 6th. The thunderstorms continued into the evening and produced damaging winds near Le Mars in Plymouth County.

A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S. during the early morning hours of the 17th.  The surface low was over South Dakota as a line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing dry line that was south of the low.  The atmosphere was moderately unstable with about 2000 J/kg as the storms developed in the evening.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only about 400 J/kg with a freezing level around 13,000 feet.  The lifted index was in the -4 to -5 C range as the storms approached with 40 to 45 kts of shear available.  The LCL was quite low, around 750 meters.  CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer was 50 to 100 J/kg.  Precipitable water values were quite high, in the 1.25 to 1.5 inch range.  For the most part, the thunderstorms were not severe and produced mainly heavy rainfall.  Several reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall were received from southwest into north central Iowa.  As the line advanced eastward, a small mesoscale vorticity maximum was observed riding north along the line.  The thunderstorms became enhanced as it moved north with a line of damage extending from Clarke County north to Webster County.  Eighty two cars of a railroad train were derailed by the winds, with two of them blown off into the nearby field.  Numerous very large trees were downed and some farm buildings lost roofs.  Reports of trees and power lines being blown down continued north as the vorticity maximum lifted north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor tree damage was reported and shingles were torn from roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S. during the early morning hours of the 17th.  The surface low was over South Dakota as a line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing dry line that was south of the low.  The atmosphere was moderately unstable with about 2000 J/kg as the storms developed in the evening.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only about 400 J/kg with a freezing level around 13,000 feet.  The lifted index was in the -4 to -5 C range as the storms approached with 40 to 45 kts of shear available.  The LCL was quite low, around 750 meters.  CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer was 50 to 100 J/kg.  Precipitable water values were quite high, in the 1.25 to 1.5 inch range.  For the most part, the thunderstorms were not severe and produced mainly heavy rainfall.  Several reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall were received from southwest into north central Iowa.  As the line advanced eastward, a small mesoscale vorticity maximum was observed riding north along the line.  The thunderstorms became enhanced as it moved north with a line of damage extending from Clarke County north to Webster County.  Eighty two cars of a railroad train were derailed by the winds, with two of them blown off into the nearby field.  Numerous very large trees were downed and some farm buildings lost roofs.  Reports of trees and power lines being blown down continued north as the vorticity maximum lifted north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farm buildings were damaged or destroyed in the 1675 to 1700 blocks of Highway 34.  A 2 X 10 was driven into the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly northward moving boundary/warm front was draped across the area throughout the day.  By the evening vigorous storms fired up just north of the boundary and continued as the low level jet began to pick up.  Numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Fairly quickly after sunset, storms transitioned away from multi-cellular severe to training heavy rain producers over an area NW to SE roughly from Carroll to Bloomfield, including portions of the Des Moines metro.  Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported large trees snapped around 2 to 3 feet in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS rolled through northern Iowa in the AM hours bringing with it numerous reports of high/damaging winds and some hail. The damaging winds brought down large tree limbs, power lines, large trees and damaged chicken and hog facilities. Lightning struck a house in Kossuth County damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported winds estimated at 70 mph.  Multiple tree limbs down in the area as well.  The best measured winds were from a personal weather station with poor siting at 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front and associated surface trough moved through the state and stalled across the southern portions of the state, firing off a few storms in southern/southwest Iowa. One storm produced damaging winds in Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs down at least 6 inches in diameter.  Big enough to require the use of a tractor/vehicle to move them.  Delayed report, time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the state during the day, dragging behind it a cold front that helped trigger off storms across portions of central and southern Iowa. Hail, damaging winds and a tornado occurred with the storms. The tornado moved from Monroe County into Wapello County damaging multiple farmsteads, power poles and a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chaser reported large trees down across roads just north of Ottumwa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moved into the state in the early morning hours from Minnesota, producing heavy rainfall across large portions of north-central Iowa.  Highest amounts reported were generally in the 4-4.50 range with a 4.60 report in Winnebago county.  The MCS progressed generally S and SSE across central Iowa through the morning hours and was able to strengthen enough by the early afternoon to produce wind damage reports in far south central Iowa, including a few large trees, overturned campers, and downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 4 inch diameter tree branches down in and around Moulton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active early June weather pattern continued into the day on June 6th. Warm front lifted northward from Kansas and Missouri into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. This allowed for moisture to return to the region and instability to increase. At the same time a storm system moving across the northern Plains allowed for a cold front to move into the region. Thunderstorms developed by mid to to late afternoon at the intersection of where these fronts met. As these thunderstorms moved east and southeast across the area during the evening isolated severe hail and strong were reported with this line. There were also periods of heavy rainfall observed in northeast Nebraska as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer reported winds to near 60 mph. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southeast into Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of the 3rd.  The atmosphere became quite unstable with temperatures rising into the low to mid 80s and dewpoints in the 55 to 60 degree range.  As thunderstorms began to form along the front, CAPE had risen to around 2000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 300 and 500 J/kg. The freezing level was between 11,500 and 12,000 feet.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 1000 to 1200 J/kg range.  Though many parameters were favorable, the shear was limited with 25 to 30 kts of effective shear available.  Thunderstorms formed along the cold front and became severe relatively quickly.  The main severe weather with the storms was hail with most of the hail reported around quarter size.  Hail covered the ground in a few areas.  Nearly baseball size hail fell in Calhoun County at Manson.  The storm in Algona produced a 59 MPH wind gust at the airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof reported blown off school building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight and morning rainfall was heavy, totally in excess of 2 inches in many locations, including high values of 4.2 and 6.4 respectively in the State Center area.  A couple instances of flash flooding were observed as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 18 inch diameter tree downed along with smaller 4 inch branches down nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary was pushing through the state with ongoing convection through central Iowa through the day. As the boundary neared the area, the convection intensified and impacted central and southern Iowa in the evening to early morning hours. Two thunderstorm complexes moved through portions of central and southern Iowa with very heavy rainfall causing flash flooding. The severe thunderstorm complexes produced some hail but mainly strong damaging winds. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches in some areas with deep warm cloud depths during the event. This heavy rainfall further exacerbated conditions as some of the area had just received heavy rainfall in previous days. This led to some extended flooding for a few days and flooding on some area rivers. Several locations reported in excess of 4 inches of rain, while one location in Warren County reported over 7.50 inches of rainfall. The Emergency Manager for Marion County mentioned widespread reports of 8 to 10 inches with one report close to 12 inches of accumulated rainfall over the past few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported numerous tree limbs down on houses and powerlines.  Time estimated from radar. An estimated 14 inch diameter tree was snapped near the base. Power was out in portions of town.

Thunderstorms caused damaging winds at several locations in northwest Iowa from just before to a little after sunrise on the morning of June 22nd. There was also one report of penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds destroyed an airport hangar, and destroyed or damaged 5 planes in the hangar. The winds caused widespread tree damage, including numerous trees blown down or completely uprooted. The tree damage included many trees at a local golf course. The winds damaged homes and buildings in and near Sheldon, including one home that was damaged by a large falling tree. A large silo was damaged and several small farm buildings were destroyed. The farm damage included a large shed destroyed and machinery inside the shed damaged. A commercial building under construction was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging winds at Little Rock in Lyon County of far northwest Iowa on the evening of June 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including several large trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across northeast Iowa on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and at least one tornado. The tornado destroyed one farm house near Maynard (Fayette County) causing injuries to both occupants of the house. Several other nearby farms sustained damage to crops, trees, and barns.  As the squall line moved across northeast Iowa it produced a swath of wind damage starting near Marble Rock (Floyd County) to around Guttenberg (Clayton County). Within this swath, a 65 mph wind gust was measured in Marble Rock that took down trees and power lines, around 17 farms sustained damage across southern Fayette County, and an Amish grocery store was destroyed near Garber (Clayton County). Preliminary damage estimates for Fayette County are in excess of $3 million. Locally heavy rains also produced some flash flooding with 480th Street washed out south of Stacyville (Floyd County) and Waterloo Creek came out of its banks near Dorchester (Allamakee County) within 30 minutes of the rain starting. A federal disaster declaration was made for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system fired areas of thunderstorms with some damaging wind reported late in the afternoon in far southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the center of town, a few older trees 2 feet in diameter were snapped off, local power was out, debris was everywhere. Up to 100 trees in town were damaged or down in the town of Keosauqua.  The time of the event is estimated from local radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system fired areas of thunderstorms with some damaging wind reported late in the afternoon in far southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were confirmed down on 205th Street in Pittsburg.  The time was estimated  from local radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave on a warm front fired several rounds of thunderstorms in an moderately unstable air mass.  A couple of damaging wind events were reported with these storms in southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few 20 to 25 inch diameter trees were uprooted.  Roof damage to a structure was reported.  Numerous tree branches up to 10 inches in diameter were down.  This was a delayed report with time estimated from 720 to 740 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly northward moving boundary/warm front was draped across the area throughout the day.  By the evening vigorous storms fired up just north of the boundary and continued as the low level jet began to pick up.  Numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Fairly quickly after sunset, storms transitioned away from multi-cellular severe to training heavy rain producers over an area NW to SE roughly from Carroll to Bloomfield, including portions of the Des Moines metro.  Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Official reported power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms from the late morning through the evening hours June 20th, produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rains.  In addition 4 tornadoes occurred.  One tornado resulted in 2 injuries when a house was destroyed.  Another person was injured by a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported multiple 3 inch diameter limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening thunderstorm complex moved into western Iowa early in the morning on May 3rd. The light rain falling from the weakening thunderstorms interacted with a strong low-level jet to allow for strong winds to translate to the surface creating strong winds gusts.

An MCS moved into the state in the early morning hours from Minnesota, producing heavy rainfall across large portions of north-central Iowa.  Highest amounts reported were generally in the 4-4.50 range with a 4.60 report in Winnebago county.  The MCS progressed generally S and SSE across central Iowa through the morning hours and was able to strengthen enough by the early afternoon to produce wind damage reports in far south central Iowa, including a few large trees, overturned campers, and downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported a camper turned over at Rathbun Lake - Island View Park.  Time was estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms moved over Iowa, Illinois, and northern Missouri from the early morning hours through the late evening hours. Many locations saw damaging winds during this multiple round event. with locations near the city of Cedar Rapids seeing a very large hail event.

Several rounds of severe thunderstorms moved over Iowa, Illinois, and northern Missouri from the early morning hours through the late evening hours. Many locations saw damaging winds during this multiple round event. with locations near the city of Cedar Rapids seeing a very large hail event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High school wind equipment on west of of Independence recorded a 93.9 mph wind gusts. Other very high winds in the area support this possibly being a correct measurement on an elevated surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary was pushing through the state with ongoing convection through central Iowa through the day. As the boundary neared the area, the convection intensified and impacted central and southern Iowa in the evening to early morning hours. Two thunderstorm complexes moved through portions of central and southern Iowa with very heavy rainfall causing flash flooding. The severe thunderstorm complexes produced some hail but mainly strong damaging winds. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches in some areas with deep warm cloud depths during the event. This heavy rainfall further exacerbated conditions as some of the area had just received heavy rainfall in previous days. This led to some extended flooding for a few days and flooding on some area rivers. Several locations reported in excess of 4 inches of rain, while one location in Warren County reported over 7.50 inches of rainfall. The Emergency Manager for Marion County mentioned widespread reports of 8 to 10 inches with one report close to 12 inches of accumulated rainfall over the past few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was down on a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across northeast Iowa on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and at least one tornado. The tornado destroyed one farm house near Maynard (Fayette County) causing injuries to both occupants of the house. Several other nearby farms sustained damage to crops, trees, and barns.  As the squall line moved across northeast Iowa it produced a swath of wind damage starting near Marble Rock (Floyd County) to around Guttenberg (Clayton County). Within this swath, a 65 mph wind gust was measured in Marble Rock that took down trees and power lines, around 17 farms sustained damage across southern Fayette County, and an Amish grocery store was destroyed near Garber (Clayton County). Preliminary damage estimates for Fayette County are in excess of $3 million. Locally heavy rains also produced some flash flooding with 480th Street washed out south of Stacyville (Floyd County) and Waterloo Creek came out of its banks near Dorchester (Allamakee County) within 30 minutes of the rain starting. The State of Iowa Governor issued a disaster proclamation for the following counties: Allamakee, Clayton, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek. A federal disaster declaration was made for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and the power knocked out in Postville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved slowly southeast across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the day on July 24th. Ahead of the front, isolated thunderstorms developed during the early morning on the 24th as moisture returned to the region. Later in the day, extreme instability developed ahead of the front as temperatures soared into the upper 90s coupled with dew points in the mid and upper 70s. Two rounds of thunderstorms developed in association with the front. The first was in the late afternoon over east central Nebraska into southwest Iowa, and then a second round occurred as storms moved into the region from central Nebraska around midnight crossing into southwest Iowa early in the morning on the 25th. Both of these rounds produced isolated severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated wind gusts to 60 mph.

Widespread severe thunderstorms from the late morning through the evening hours June 20th, produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rains.  In addition 4 tornadoes occurred.  One tornado resulted in 2 injuries when a house was destroyed.  Another person was injured by a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches between 6 and 10 inches in diameter were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary was pushing through the state with ongoing convection through central Iowa through the day. As the boundary neared the area, the convection intensified and impacted central and southern Iowa in the evening to early morning hours. Two thunderstorm complexes moved through portions of central and southern Iowa with very heavy rainfall causing flash flooding. The severe thunderstorm complexes produced some hail but mainly strong damaging winds. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches in some areas with deep warm cloud depths during the event. This heavy rainfall further exacerbated conditions as some of the area had just received heavy rainfall in previous days. This led to some extended flooding for a few days and flooding on some area rivers. Several locations reported in excess of 4 inches of rain, while one location in Warren County reported over 7.50 inches of rainfall. The Emergency Manager for Marion County mentioned widespread reports of 8 to 10 inches with one report close to 12 inches of accumulated rainfall over the past few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on an apartment building. Four of the apartments had to be evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level storm system moved into the northern Plains, a warm front retreated through eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the afternoon. Unseasonably warm and humid air lifted into the region creating marginal instability by mid afternoon, but with the warm front in the area environmental shear was strong. Thunderstorms developed across northeast and east-central Nebraska by mid afternoon. Initially isolated supercells were the dominate mode with large hail the main threat. A small short-lived tornado did develop from one of the supercells in Washington County. Thunderstorms then moved into western Iowa as they continued to produce large hail. By mid evening the thunderstorms developed a common outflow in a west to east orientation and allowed for training, especially over northeast Nebraska. This continued regeneration of thunderstorms over the same area led to significant river and flash flooding over parts of the through the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by a Iowa State department of transportation road weather sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively weak warm front moved through portions of the state through the day, with temperatures into the low to mid 80s and dewpoints into the lower 60s.  This day was highlighted as a relatively high CAPE and low shear event.  As a result, storms did not have much structure to them and often pulsed up and down rather quickly.  The dominant severe threat was hail with an opportunity for severe winds as storms collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire department reported powerlines down near the 190th street area of H ave west of Minburn.  Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of May 26th across northeast Iowa. These storms formed ahead of a cold front and produced strong winds across portions of Mitchell, Fayette and Clayton Counties. In Oelwein (Fayette County), a 62 mph wind gust was measured by the automated weather observing equipment at the airport. Several barns were damaged by the high winds near Arlington (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down and trees damaged in Monona.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roofs were damaged or blown off barns and some hog confinements in the Arlington area. One barn was blown over by the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms worked their way across Nebraska overnight on the 18th into the 19th, eventually making their way into Iowa. As those elevated storms approached the central third of Iowa, the linear storms began to backbuild and with increasing convection behind them in association with ongoing warm air advection.  Through the morning hours ample lightning was associated with the storms along with heavy rainfall around 3 inches in the Fort Dodge area and 4 to 6 inches in the Des Moines area. A number of flash flooding events were reported as a result of the heavy rainfall.  During the afternoon, the storms resumed their forward propagation and increased in strength, especially across southern and southeast Iowa.  Damaging winds and a couple of tornado reports were recorded across south central Iowa along with rainfall in the 1 to 3 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported 8 to 10 inch diameter tree limbs blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Through much of the day of the 16th, a warm front slowly progressed into the state from the west, orienting itself north-south about a third to half of the way into the state.  As a result, storms were able to initiate and provide periods of heavy rainfall, numerous damaging wind reports, and a handful of severe hail reports.  Heavy rainfall amounts approached 2 to 3 inches across northern Iowa. The majority of severe wind reports occurred between 2 am and 4:30 am, heavy rainfall reports after around 7 am, and hail reports during redevelopment during the mid-late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported several power lines and large trees down throughout the town of Alden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Through the day of the 5th, a warm front lifted through the region increasing temperatures and dew points, priming conditions for an impending cold front.  That evening, as the low pressure center lifted into southern Minnesota, the trailing cold front entered the state. It initiated storms across northeast Iowa that remained sub-severe while in the Des Moines WFO service area, as well as a series of storms in eastern Nebraska that eventually made their way across central Iowa.  Those storms primarily produced damaging winds and severe wind gusts along with periods of heavy rainfall.  After the initial cluster of storms moved through and weakened overnight, new development along the sagging cold front produced a morning round of damaging winds and heavy rainfall across southern and southeast Iowa.  Lastly, during the afternoon, conditions were primed for possible funnel cloud/landspout development, and was seen in Tama County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Home weather station recorded 62 mph wind gust.
